planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; golden angel's trumpet produces large, pendant, golden-yellow, trumpet-shaped flowers and is one of the most spectacular large-flowered tropical shrubs. CAUTION: All parts of all Brugmansia species are extremely toxic — do not plant where children or pets have unsupervised access.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; golden angel's trumpet is an Andean species producing enormous, pendant, trumpet-shaped flowers up to 12 inches long in soft golden-yellow, often with a rich, sweet, tropical fragrance strongest in the evening. A single well-grown plant can carry dozens of pendant blooms simultaneously, creating a spectacular tropical accent. Like all Brugmansia, all plant parts are dangerously toxic — containing tropane alkaloids (scopolamine, atropine) that cause hallucinations, rapid heartbeat, and potentially fatal poisoning. Never use any plant part medicinally without expert guidance. In zone 9, grow as a container plant moved indoors in winter or in a frost-protected location. Fertilize heavily with a balanced fertilizer every 2 weeks during the growing season to achieve maximum bloom production. The large, pendant flowers are adapted for pollination by large hawk moths.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; white angel's trumpet is the most widely cultivated Brugmansia species with the strongest fragrance. CAUTION: All parts are extremely toxic. Do not plant where children or pets have unsupervised access.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; white angel's trumpet is the most fragrant and widely grown Brugmansia species, producing pendant, white, trumpet-shaped flowers up to 12 inches long with an intensely sweet, heady tropical fragrance most pronounced in the evening — one of the most powerfully scented flowers of any tropical plant. The fragrance on a warm summer evening can perfume an entire garden. Multiple flushes of bloom occur throughout the warm season. Like all Brugmansia, all parts are dangerously toxic — do not grow where children or curious pets have access. In zones 9–10, it grows as a large semi-evergreen shrub; in zone 8 it dies to the ground in winter and re-sprouts from the base. Heavy feeding with balanced fertilizer and consistent moisture produce the largest and most prolific flowers. Widely available in both white and cream-to-pale-yellow double-flowered forms.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, average to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; scarlet morning glory is a vigorous warm-season annual vine native to tropical Americas that self-sows freely in zone 9 and establishes quickly in any sunny, warm location. Scarify seeds for faster germination.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, average to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; scarlet morning glory (hummingbird vine) produces small, brilliant scarlet, tubular flowers with a yellow throat on vigorous twining stems — like a more compact, finer-textured version of the large garden morning glories, but with a much more intensely colored, tubular flower that is irresistible to hummingbirds and monarch butterflies. The distinctive ivy-shaped leaves (hederifolia = ivy-leaved) distinguish it from other red-flowered morning glories. Native to tropical Americas and naturalized throughout the subtropical US, it self-sows freely in zone 9 gardens where it provides a season-long display on fences, trellises, and shrubs. More drought-tolerant than many morning glories once established. An excellent hummingbird plant for warm-climate gardens that provides continuous summer color with minimal care.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; varicolored angel's trumpet is notable for flowers that change color as they age — typically opening white and gradually turning peach, apricot, or orange. CAUTION: All parts extremely toxic.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; varicolored angel's trumpet is an Ecuadorian species named for its distinctive flower color change — the enormous pendant trumpets open white and gradually transition through cream, peach, and apricot to a final orange tone as they age, so a single plant displays multiple flower colors simultaneously, creating an exceptional multicolor effect rare in tropical ornamentals. The flowers, up to 14 inches long among the largest in the genus, have a pleasant, sweet fragrance. Like all Brugmansia, all parts are dangerously toxic and the plant must be sited away from children and animals. The enormous, tropical-looking foliage and dramatic pendant flowers create the most architectural and dramatic tropical statement in the warm-climate garden. Heavy fertilization and consistent moisture are essential for maximum performance; drought or nutrient stress cause immediate flower drop.

planting.note: Plant fresh seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; African mahogany grows naturally in the Sahel and savanna belt of West Africa in seasonally dry, tropical conditions and is more drought-tolerant than the American mahoganies once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zone 10–12; African mahogany is a West African timber and shade tree in the Meliaceae family (same family as American mahogany), widely planted in tropical and subtropical regions for shade and timber. The pinnate compound leaves create a dense canopy that provides excellent shade. The reddish-brown wood is harder than West Indian mahogany but less prized in fine furniture; it is extensively used in flooring, general construction, and plywood production in Africa. In south Florida, African mahogany is widely planted as a fast-growing street and shade tree, developing a large, spreading crown that provides dense summer shade. More salt-tolerant and drought-tolerant than American mahogany and faster-growing, making it practical for large-scale urban tree planting in zone 10 coastal Florida.
